enrichment.short_description: Cider is a free, open-source, cross-platform client for Apple Music built with Electron and Vue.js by the volunteer Cider Collective. It gives Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android users a customizable, resource-conscious alternative to Apple's official Music app while streaming through an existing Apple Music subscription via Apple's MusicKit APIs.

enrichment.seo_title: Cider Review 2026: Free Open-Source Apple Music Client
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cider is a free, open-source Apple Music client for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. Explore features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cider is a free, open-source desktop and mobile client for Apple Music, developed by a volunteer group of developers known as the Cider Collective. The project began as an indie effort called Apple Music Electron, aiming to bring Apple's streaming service to platforms Apple does not officially support, and was later rebranded as Cider. Cider 1, the original release, was built using Electron.js, Vue.js 2, and Webpack, and its source code was published as open source, allowing anyone to inspect, modify, or self-host the client. Cider connects to a user's existing Apple Music account through Apple's official MusicKit JS and MusicKit APIs, meaning it does not bypass Apple's licensing or DRM; a paid Apple Music subscription is still required to stream music, since Apple Music has no free tier. The application is available for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, filling a long-standing gap for Apple Music on Linux desktops, a platform Apple has never officially supported. Cider differentiates itself from Apple's stock Music app through deep customization: users can apply community themes, adjust the interface layout, and extend functionality with plugins. Notable built-in features include Discord Rich Presence integration so friends can see what a user is currently listening to, Last.fm scrobbling for tracking listening history, an audio equalizer with presets, an audio spatialization and enhancement engine sometimes referred to as an Adrenaline Processor, offline playback for downloaded songs, albums, and playlists, and support for Apple Music Sing, Apple's real-time lyrics and karaoke feature. A significant shift occurred with Cider 2, a from-scratch rewrite that moved to a high-performance custom backend built in Rust for lower memory usage and faster startup times; unlike Cider 1, Cider 2 is closed-source and proprietary, and as of 2026 remains in early access, distributed through the Cider Collective's own website, itch.io, the Microsoft Store, and other channels. This licensing split is an important distinction for anyone evaluating Cider for its open-source credentials: Cider 1's codebase remains publicly available on GitHub under the Cider Collective organization, while Cider 2's binaries are free to use but not open source. The Cider Collective funds ongoing development through optional, pay-what-you-want contributions on itch.io (with a suggested price point around 3.49 dollars) and through Patreon, but the application itself is not gated behind a paywall. Because Cider relies on Apple's official APIs rather than reverse-engineered access, it is generally more stable across Apple Music catalog and metadata changes than unofficial scraping-based clients, though it remains dependent on Apple continuing to expose MusicKit endpoints to third parties. Cider is best understood as a community-built shell around Apple Music's official web technologies, prized by Linux users, power users who want scriptable and skinnable music players, and anyone who finds Apple's first-party Music app on Windows underdeveloped. It should not be confused with unrelated products that also use the word cider, such as beverage brands or other software projects; this Cider is specifically the Apple Music player maintained by the Cider Collective.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cider Is and Who Built It
paragraphs: Cider is an unofficial, third-party client for Apple Music, created because Apple does not provide a native Apple Music application for Windows in the same polished form as on macOS, and provides no official desktop client at all for Linux. It began life as a small project called Apple Music Electron and evolved into the Cider Collective, an open-source organization dedicated to building Apple Music software across platforms using Apple's own MusicKit and related SDKs.

Because Cider authenticates against a user's real Apple Music account and streams through Apple's sanctioned APIs, it functions as a skin and feature layer on top of the official service rather than a way to access music without paying for a subscription. This keeps Cider compliant with Apple's terms while still giving the collective room to add features Apple's own apps lack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cider 1 versus Cider 2
paragraphs: Cider 1, the version most associated with Cider's open-source reputation, was written in Electron, Vue.js 2, and Webpack, and its source is publicly hosted so the community can audit, fork, or contribute to it. This version established Cider's cross-platform reach across Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android.

Cider 2 represents a ground-up rewrite that swaps the backend for a custom Rust implementation aimed at reducing the memory footprint and CPU usage that Electron-based apps are often criticized for. Unlike its predecessor, Cider 2 is proprietary and closed-source, and as of 2026 is still labeled early access, so some features and platforms may be incomplete or unstable while it matures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why Listeners Choose Cider Over Apple's Official App
paragraphs: The most common motivation is platform availability: Apple has never shipped a native Apple Music desktop app for Linux, so Cider is one of the only ways Linux users can access their Apple Music library with a graphical interface rather than a browser tab. On Windows, users often turn to Cider because Apple's own Windows Music app has historically lagged behind the macOS and iOS experience in design and feature parity.

Beyond platform gaps, Cider appeals to users who want visual customization through themes and layout options, integrations like Discord presence and Last.fm scrobbling that Apple's app does not offer, and a lighter, more configurable listening experience overall.

faqs.question: Is Cider free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Cider is free to download and use. The Cider Collective offers optional pay-what-you-want support through itch.io and Patreon, but there is no mandatory purchase to use the core application.

faqs.question: Do I still need an Apple Music subscription to use Cider
faqs.answer: Yes. Cider is a client, not a music source, and it streams through Apple's official MusicKit APIs, so an active Apple Music subscription is required to play content.

faqs.question: Is Cider available for Linux
faqs.answer: Yes, Linux support is one of Cider's main selling points, since Apple does not offer an official Apple Music desktop app for Linux.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cider 1 and Cider 2
faqs.answer: Cider 1 is the original Electron, Vue.js, and Webpack based client with fully open-source code. Cider 2 is a rewrite with a custom Rust backend aimed at better performance, but it is closed-source and, as of 2026, still in early access.

faqs.question: Is Cider affiliated with Apple
faqs.answer: No. Cider is an independent, third-party client built by the volunteer Cider Collective. It uses Apple's public MusicKit APIs but is not developed or endorsed by Apple.

faqs.question: Is Cider open source
faqs.answer: Cider 1's source code is publicly available and open source under the Cider Collective's GitHub organization. Cider 2 is proprietary and not open source.

faqs.question: Does Cider support offline listening
faqs.answer: Yes, Cider supports downloading songs, albums, and playlists for offline playback, similar to Apple's official mobile apps.

faqs.question: Can Cider show what I am listening to on Discord
faqs.answer: Yes, Cider includes built-in Discord Rich Presence integration that can automatically display the current track on a user's Discord profile.

enrichment.short_description: Cinelerra-CV, short for Cinelerra Community Version, is a free and open-source non-linear video editor and compositor for Linux. It was forked in 2003 from Heroine Virtual's original Cinelerra codebase by community developers who wanted a version built with standard shared system libraries rather than bundled dependencies.
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer open-source community project (the Cinelerra-CV Team); not a commercial company
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable, distributed open-source project with no corporate headquarters
enrichment.seo_title: Cinelerra-CV: Free Open-Source Linux Video Editor Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cinelerra-CV is a free, open-source non-linear video editor for Linux. Learn its history, features, pros, cons, and how it differs from other forks.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cinelerra-CV, where CV stands for Community Version, is a free and open-source non-linear video editing and compositing application for Linux. It traces its origins to Cinelerra, an advanced NLE originally developed by Heroine Virtual, a small studio led by Adam Williams. Heroine Virtual's Cinelerra gained notice as one of the first video editors rewritten for 64-bit processors, being adapted for the AMD Opteron architecture in 2003 and presented at SIGGRAPH 2004. That same year, in April 2003, a group within the Cinelerra user community decided to maintain an independent copy of the software's source code, giving rise to Cinelerra-CV. The relationship between Heroine Virtual and the Cinelerra-CV community has generally been described as cooperative rather than adversarial, with Heroine Virtual occasionally participating in Cinelerra-CV mailing list discussions and incorporating some community-driven changes back into its own line. The key technical distinction driving the fork is dependency management: Cinelerra-CV is built to compile against libraries already installed as shared system libraries on a user's Linux distribution, whereas Heroine Virtual's official branch, sometimes called Cinelerra-HV, bundles many of its own library versions directly into its custom build process. This makes Cinelerra-CV easier to package and maintain for Linux distributions and system integrators, even though it is derived from the same original codebase. Cinelerra-CV should not be confused with the other major Cinelerra descendants that emerged later: Cinelerra-GG Infinity, maintained by Good Guy (William Morrow), which has become one of the more actively developed forks with modern hardware acceleration support, and Lumiera, an ambitious from-scratch redesign inspired by Cinelerra's editing model that never reached a stable production release. As a non-linear editor, Cinelerra-CV supports timeline-based editing across multiple video and audio tracks, track-based compositing operations such as keying and mattes, a built-in title generator, a library of video and audio effects, and keyframe automation for animating effect parameters over time. It is aimed at users who need professional-grade editing and compositing capabilities on Linux without relying on proprietary software, and it has historically been popular among Linux enthusiasts, hobbyist filmmakers, and technically inclined video professionals willing to work with a less polished, community-maintained interface in exchange for full source access and no licensing cost. Cinelerra-CV's official code repository has been hosted on GitHub under the Cinelerra-CV Team organization since around 2020, when the project's official site and repository were refreshed, and it continues to receive maintenance updates including bug fixes and compiler compatibility improvements from a small group of volunteer contributors. Because development activity is comparatively modest relative to more actively funded commercial editors, users evaluating Cinelerra-CV in 2026 should expect an interface and workflow that reflects its long history rather than a modern redesign, and should weigh it against sibling forks like Cinelerra-GG if broader hardware acceleration or more frequent updates are a priority.

long_form_blocks.heading: History and the 2003 Community Fork
paragraphs: Cinelerra originated with Heroine Virtual and Adam Williams, and became notable as an early 64-bit-capable video editor when it was adapted for the AMD Opteron processor in 2003. In April of that same year, members of the surrounding user community chose to maintain their own copy of the source code, creating Cinelerra-CV as a parallel, community-governed branch.

Rather than becoming a hostile fork, Cinelerra-CV developed alongside Heroine Virtual's official line with a generally cooperative relationship, including some cross-pollination of fixes and discussion between the two camps over the years.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Cinelerra-CV Differs From Other Forks
paragraphs: The defining technical choice behind Cinelerra-CV is its use of standard shared system libraries wherever possible, making it more straightforward to build and package for Linux distributions compared to Heroine Virtual's original approach of bundling custom library versions.

Cinelerra-CV is one of several descendants of the original Cinelerra codebase alongside Cinelerra-GG Infinity, a more actively developed fork with added hardware acceleration and modern codec support, and Lumiera, a separate reimplementation effort. Users researching Cinelerra products should confirm which specific fork a guide or package refers to, since features and stability differ meaningfully between them.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Editing and Compositing Capabilities
paragraphs: Cinelerra-CV provides multi-track timeline editing, track-based compositing with keying and matte support, a title generator for on-screen text, and a set of built-in audio and video effects that can be automated through keyframes.

These capabilities position it as a professional-oriented tool rather than a simplified consumer editor, though its interface and workflow reflect design decisions made in the early 2000s rather than a contemporary redesign.

faqs.question: What does the CV in Cinelerra-CV stand for
faqs.answer: CV stands for Community Version, reflecting that this branch of Cinelerra is maintained by an independent community rather than by the software's original author.

faqs.question: Is Cinelerra-CV free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Cinelerra-CV is free and open source, distributed under a GPL-style license with no purchase or subscription required.

faqs.question: How is Cinelerra-CV different from Cinelerra-GG
faqs.answer: Both are forks of the original Cinelerra codebase, but Cinelerra-GG Infinity has seen more active recent development, including expanded hardware acceleration, while Cinelerra-CV emphasizes building against standard shared system libraries.

faqs.question: Does Cinelerra-CV run on Windows or macOS
faqs.answer: No, Cinelerra-CV is designed for Linux and does not have official native Windows or macOS builds.

faqs.question: When was Cinelerra-CV created
faqs.answer: The fork originated in April 2003 when community developers chose to maintain their own copy of Heroine Virtual's original Cinelerra source code.

faqs.question: Is Cinelerra-CV still maintained in 2026
faqs.answer: The project continues to be maintained by volunteer contributors under the Cinelerra-CV Team on GitHub, though at a more modest pace than commercially backed video editors.

faqs.question: What kind of compositing does Cinelerra-CV support
faqs.answer: It supports track-based compositing including keying and matte operations, commonly used for tasks like green-screen removal and layered video effects.

faqs.question: Who should consider using Cinelerra-CV
faqs.answer: It suits Linux users who want a free, professional-capable editor with compositing and keyframe automation and who are comfortable with an interface that predates most modern editing software redesigns.

enrichment.short_description: Cinnamon is a free and open-source desktop environment for Linux, developed by the Linux Mint team after GNOME 3's 2011 release replaced the traditional GNOME 2 desktop metaphor with GNOME Shell. Originally forked from GNOME Shell components in January 2012 as Project Cinnamon, it became a fully independent desktop environment with the release of Cinnamon 2.0 in October 2013.

enrichment.headquarters: Developed by the Linux Mint project, commonly associated with lead developer Clement Lefebvre; no fixed corporate headquarters
enrichment.seo_title: Cinnamon Desktop Environment: Features and 2026 Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cinnamon is a free, open-source Linux desktop environment from the Linux Mint team. Explore its history, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cinnamon is a free and open-source desktop environment for Linux, best known as the flagship, default interface of Linux Mint. Its origin traces to April 2011, when the release of GNOME 3 replaced the familiar GNOME 2 desktop paradigm with GNOME Shell, a significantly different interface that many users, including the Linux Mint team, found unsuited to a traditional desktop workflow. The Linux Mint developers initially tried to reconcile GNOME 3 with a classic desktop feel through a set of add-ons called Mint GNOME Shell Extensions, or MGSE, which shipped alongside a MATE desktop option in Linux Mint 12, released in November 2011. Finding extensions alone insufficient for the level of control they wanted, the team forked GNOME Shell's underlying components directly in January 2012, naming the effort Project Cinnamon. Cinnamon initially still relied on GNOME infrastructure as a kind of alternate frontend, similar in spirit to how Ubuntu's Unity or GNOME Shell itself functioned. That changed with the release of Cinnamon 2.0.0 on October 9, 2013, which marked Cinnamon's transition into a fully independent desktop environment that no longer requires GNOME to be installed as a dependency, even though it continues to be built heavily on GNOME-originated technologies such as GTK. Cinnamon preserves a conventional desktop metaphor: a bottom or top panel, a traditional application menu, a taskbar showing open windows, and a system tray, which appealed strongly to users migrating from Windows or from GNOME 2 who wanted continuity rather than the more mobile-influenced, activities-based workflow of GNOME Shell. Around Cinnamon, the Linux Mint team built a family of companion tools, most notably Nemo, a file manager forked from GNOME Files (formerly Nautilus) that serves as Cinnamon's default file browser and desktop shell. Nemo supports features like dual-pane browsing, a toggleable breadcrumb path bar, automatic thumbnail generation, and, since Cinnamon 6.0, an extensible actions system for customizing file manager context menus. Cinnamon itself is customizable through applets, which add functionality to the panel; desklets, which place small widgets on the desktop; extensions, which modify broader desktop behavior; and themes, all distributed and installed through a system called Cinnamon Spices. Because Cinnamon is free, open-source software, there is no licensing cost or commercial pricing tier; it is bundled by default with Linux Mint and is also packaged for installation on many other Linux distributions, including Fedora, Debian, Arch Linux, and openSUSE, for users who prefer Cinnamon's interface outside of Mint itself. Development is coordinated by the Linux Mint team, led by Clement Lefebvre, and sustained through community contributions, user donations, and sponsorships that fund the broader Linux Mint project, rather than through a traditional corporate structure. As of 2026, Cinnamon continues to receive regular feature and stability updates, including incremental work toward Wayland display server support, an area where it has historically lagged behind GNOME Shell and KDE Plasma, which completed their Wayland transitions earlier. Cinnamon remains most relevant to users who prioritize a familiar, low-friction desktop layout, configurability through applets and themes, and a lighter alternative to GNOME Shell's more opinionated workflow, while still being built on modern GTK-based GNOME technologies underneath.

long_form_blocks.heading: From GNOME Shell Extensions to an Independent Desktop
paragraphs: Cinnamon's origin is directly tied to the controversial 2011 release of GNOME 3, which replaced the classic GNOME 2 desktop with GNOME Shell's activities-based interface. The Linux Mint team, wanting to preserve a more traditional desktop workflow for its users, first attempted a lighter-touch fix through Mint GNOME Shell Extensions before deciding a full fork was necessary.

That fork, launched as Project Cinnamon in January 2012, gradually diverged from GNOME Shell's codebase until Cinnamon 2.0 in October 2013 made it a fully standalone desktop environment that no longer depends on GNOME being installed, even though it still shares GNOME's underlying GTK toolkit and many conventions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Nemo and the Cinnamon Ecosystem
paragraphs: Alongside the desktop shell itself, the Linux Mint team maintains Nemo, a file manager forked from GNOME's Nautilus, as Cinnamon's default file browser and desktop icon handler. Nemo adds features such as dual-pane browsing and a customizable actions system for right-click context menus.

Customization across Cinnamon happens through four main mechanisms: applets that live in the panel, desklets placed directly on the desktop, extensions that alter broader system behavior, and themes that restyle the interface. All of these are distributed through Cinnamon Spices, the project's central repository for community-made add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Uses Cinnamon and Why
paragraphs: Cinnamon is the default and most closely associated desktop environment of Linux Mint, one of the most widely used desktop Linux distributions, particularly among users transitioning from Windows who want a familiar taskbar-and-menu layout.

Beyond Linux Mint, Cinnamon is packaged for installation on other major distributions for users who prefer its conventional layout and GTK foundation over alternatives like GNOME Shell's activities view or KDE Plasma's more configurable but complex interface.

faqs.question: Is Cinnamon the same as Linux Mint
faqs.answer: No. Linux Mint is a Linux distribution that offers Cinnamon as its flagship default desktop environment, but Cinnamon can also be installed on other distributions like Fedora, Debian, and Arch Linux.

faqs.question: Is Cinnamon free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Cinnamon is free and open-source software with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: When was Cinnamon created
faqs.answer: Development began in 2011 following GNOME 3's release, with the formal Project Cinnamon fork of GNOME Shell components starting in January 2012 and full independence achieved with Cinnamon 2.0 in October 2013.

faqs.question: Does Cinnamon require GNOME to be installed
faqs.answer: No, since Cinnamon 2.0.0 in 2013, Cinnamon runs as a fully independent desktop environment and does not require a GNOME installation, even though it still uses GTK and other GNOME-originated technologies.

faqs.question: What file manager does Cinnamon use by default
faqs.answer: Cinnamon uses Nemo, a file manager forked from GNOME's Nautilus, as its default file browser and desktop handler.

faqs.question: Does Cinnamon support Wayland
faqs.answer: Cinnamon has been incrementally adding Wayland display server support in recent releases, though its Xorg support has historically been more mature and complete.

faqs.question: How do I customize Cinnamon
faqs.answer: Cinnamon can be customized through applets in the panel, desklets on the desktop, system-level extensions, and visual themes, all browsable through the built-in Cinnamon Spices repository.

faqs.question: Who maintains Cinnamon
faqs.answer: Cinnamon is developed by the Linux Mint team, led by Clement Lefebvre, along with a community of volunteer contributors.

enrichment.short_description: CipherCloud was a cloud security company that built a cloud access security broker, or CASB, platform providing encryption, tokenization, data loss prevention, and adaptive access controls for enterprise cloud applications such as Salesforce, Office 365, and Google Drive. Founded in 2010 and headquartered in San Jose, California, CipherCloud was acquired by Lookout in March 2021, and its CASB technology line was later sold by Lookout to Fortra in May 2025.

enrichment.seo_title: CipherCloud: CASB History, Features, and Current Status
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CipherCloud was a cloud access security broker acquired by Lookout in 2021 and later sold to Fortra. Learn its history, features, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CipherCloud was an American cybersecurity company that pioneered cloud access security broker, or CASB, technology, founded in 2010 by Pravin Kothari, who had previously co-founded the security company ArcSight. Headquartered in San Jose, California, CipherCloud launched its first commercial product in February 2011, initially focused on securing Salesforce.com deployments through encryption and tokenization applied before sensitive data left an organization's control. The company expanded quickly, adding support for Amazon Web Services environments later in 2011, releasing a Gmail security service in June 2012, and extending coverage to Force.com, Chatter, and Microsoft Office 365 by September 2012. CipherCloud's growth was backed by significant venture funding, including a 30 million dollar round led by Andreessen Horowitz and Index Ventures closed in December 2012, the same month the company opened a European headquarters in London. It later added an Australian headquarters in 2013 and made an acquisition of its own, purchasing CloudUp Networks in January 2014, followed by the launch of CipherCloud Cloud Discovery in April 2014, a product designed to analyze firewall and proxy logs to detect unsanctioned Shadow IT cloud application usage and assess associated risk. Over the following years, CipherCloud's flagship offering evolved into a product generally marketed as CipherCloud CASB Plus, which bundled data loss prevention, in-motion and at-rest encryption, digital rights management, and adaptive, risk-based access controls into a single platform. The platform was built to protect data across more than 40 major cloud applications, and included capabilities such as continuous activity monitoring across multiple cloud services with anomaly detection, real-time malware scanning and quarantine to stop threats from entering networks through cloud channels, and compliance-oriented reporting and auditing designed to help organizations meet frameworks like GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS. In March 2021, CipherCloud was acquired by Lookout, a mobile and endpoint security company, in a deal intended to combine Lookout's endpoint security with CipherCloud's cloud-native SASE-oriented technologies, spanning CASB, zero-trust network access, secure web gateway, and data loss prevention, into a single end-to-end security platform covering the path from endpoint to cloud. Financial terms of the acquisition were not disclosed, and CipherCloud's founder and CEO Pravin Kothari joined Lookout as an executive vice president overseeing product and SASE strategy. Following the acquisition, the CipherCloud brand was gradually folded into Lookout's product naming, with offerings referred to as Lookout CASB or Lookout Secure Cloud Access. In a further ownership change, Lookout sold the assets of its cloud security business, built on the former CipherCloud technology, to Fortra in May 2025. As a result, CipherCloud as an independent, standalone brand no longer exists in the market as of 2026; its technology and customer base now operate under Fortra's ownership, while the original CipherCloud company name is primarily of historical and reference interest for understanding the lineage of modern CASB and cloud data protection products. Anyone researching CipherCloud today is most likely encountering references to its historical product capabilities, its acquisition history, or comparisons made in CASB market analyses that still cite it as an early and influential player in the category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Founding and Early Product Expansion
paragraphs: CipherCloud was founded in 2010 by Pravin Kothari, a repeat security entrepreneur who had previously co-founded ArcSight. The company launched its first product in February 2011 focused on protecting Salesforce.com data through encryption and tokenization, then quickly broadened coverage to Amazon Web Services, Gmail, Office 365, Force.com, and Chatter within about eighteen months.

Strong early venture backing, including a 30 million dollar round in December 2012 led by Andreessen Horowitz and Index Ventures, funded international expansion, with new regional headquarters opened in London in 2012 and Australia in 2013.

long_form_blocks.heading: What the CipherCloud CASB Platform Did
paragraphs: CipherCloud's core platform, marketed as CipherCloud CASB Plus, combined encryption, tokenization, and data loss prevention into a single cloud access security broker offering, protecting data across more than 40 major cloud applications including Office 365, Google Drive, and Salesforce.

The platform also included adaptive, risk-based access controls, cross-cloud activity monitoring with anomaly detection, real-time malware scanning, and compliance reporting for frameworks such as GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS, positioning it for regulated enterprise customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Acquisition by Lookout and Later Sale to Fortra
paragraphs: Lookout acquired CipherCloud in March 2021 to pair its endpoint security business with CipherCloud's cloud-native SASE technologies, aiming to offer protection across the full path from endpoint to cloud. CipherCloud's founder Pravin Kothari joined Lookout in an executive role overseeing the combined product and strategy.

In May 2025, Lookout sold the assets of its cloud security business, which had been built on CipherCloud's technology, to Fortra. As a result, the CipherCloud name no longer represents an independently operating company as of 2026, and its former product line now sits within Fortra's security portfolio.

enrichment.seo_title: cirun.io Review 2026: Self-Hosted CI Runners on Your Cloud
enrichment.seo_meta_description: cirun.io provisions self-hosted GitHub Actions and GitLab CI runners on your own cloud account. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: cirun.io is a continuous integration and continuous delivery infrastructure tool that solves a specific, common problem for engineering teams: GitHub Actions and GitLab CI's own hosted runners are convenient but can become slow or expensive for larger builds, especially workloads that need more CPU, memory, GPU access, or ARM architecture support than the vendor-hosted default runners provide. Rather than operating its own fleet of build machines and billing per minute the way traditional hosted CI providers do, cirun.io orchestrates self-hosted runners on infrastructure the customer already owns and pays for directly, including Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, Oracle Cloud, DigitalOcean, and on-premise OpenStack environments. Teams define runner requirements, such as instance type, region, and cloud provider, in a repository configuration file named dot-cirun-dot-yml, and cirun.io handles provisioning an ephemeral virtual machine for each CI job, registering it as a runner, and tearing it down automatically once the job completes. Because the underlying compute is billed directly by the cloud provider rather than marked up by cirun.io, organizations can access spot or reserved pricing, custom machine images, and specialized hardware like GPUs that are difficult or impossible to get from GitHub's or GitLab's own hosted runner tiers, while cirun.io itself charges only for the orchestration layer. cirun.io is developed by Aktech Labs, a boutique engineering consultancy based in London, United Kingdom, that works across web development, machine learning, big data, and operations research; the product appears to have emerged from Aktech Labs' own infrastructure needs before becoming a public offering, with visible activity dating back to around 2021. The GitHub account associated with the project is maintained under the AktechLabs organization, and Amit Kumar is publicly associated with the project's GitHub presence. cirun.io positions itself as sustainable and independently operated rather than venture-funded, describing itself as financially self-sufficient and growing through customer revenue, which the company frames as allowing it to prioritize user and developer community needs over investor pressure. Pricing follows a tiered subscription model gated by the number of connected private repositories: an Open Source tier is free and includes unlimited public repositories, unlimited runners, and access to Arm, GPU, and all supported cloud providers; a Startup tier at 29 dollars per month supports up to 3 private repositories with Slack and email support; a Business tier at 79 dollars per month supports up to 10 private repositories; an Enterprise tier at 99 dollars per month supports up to 20 private repositories; and an Enterprise Plus tier at 499 dollars per month supports up to 100 private repositories with premium support. In every tier, the actual compute costs for the provisioned virtual machines are billed separately by the customer's chosen cloud provider, not by cirun.io. This model makes cirun.io most attractive to teams already running meaningful workloads on cloud infrastructure who want tighter cost control, custom hardware access such as GPU-enabled runners for machine learning pipelines, or compliance requirements that mandate builds run inside their own cloud account rather than a third party's shared infrastructure, as opposed to smaller teams or hobby projects for whom GitHub's or GitLab's default hosted runners remain simpler and sufficient.

long_form_blocks.heading: What cirun.io Does
paragraphs: cirun.io orchestrates self-hosted CI runners for GitHub Actions and GitLab CI, provisioning ephemeral virtual machines on a customer's own cloud account for each job and tearing them down once the job finishes. This differs fundamentally from GitHub's or GitLab's own hosted runners, which run on infrastructure owned and billed by GitHub or GitLab directly.

Configuration happens through a repository file, dot-cirun-dot-yml, where teams specify labels tied to instance types, regions, and cloud providers, letting individual workflow jobs request exactly the hardware they need, including GPU or ARM-based machines.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Pricing and Billing Work
paragraphs: cirun.io charges a subscription fee for its orchestration service based on the number of private repositories connected, while public and open-source repositories are supported free of charge with unlimited runners across supported cloud providers.

Separately, the actual virtual machine compute time is billed directly by the customer's cloud provider of choice, meaning organizations retain full visibility and control over their cloud spend and can apply existing discounts, reserved instances, or spot pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Background
paragraphs: cirun.io is built and maintained by Aktech Labs, a London-based engineering consultancy that also takes on client work spanning web development, machine learning, big data, and operations research.

The project is described as financially self-sustaining, funded by customer revenue rather than outside venture investment, which the team presents as a deliberate choice to keep product decisions focused on user needs.

enrichment.seo_title: Clapper Media Player: Free GNOME Video Player Guide 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clapper is a free, open-source GTK4 media player for GNOME built on GStreamer. See features, pros, cons, and FAQs for this modern Linux video player.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clapper is a free, open-source media player built specifically for the GNOME desktop environment, using the GTK4 toolkit for its interface and the GStreamer multimedia framework as its playback backend, with OpenGL used for rendering. The project is coded primarily in GJS, GNOME's JavaScript bindings, and is developed largely by a single developer, Rafal Dzieciol known by the handle Rafostar, who maintains the project mainly as a personal, hobby-driven effort in his free time, which the project itself acknowledges results in steady rather than especially fast development pace. Clapper's visual identity departs from conventional media player design by omitting a traditional upper title bar in favor of auto-hiding controls, including a preferences menu, a mode switcher, and window control buttons that appear only when needed, giving the interface an unusually clean, minimalist look when paused or during full-screen playback. The player offers three distinct viewing modes: a standard Windowed Mode for normal desktop use, a Floating Mode that keeps a resizable video window on top of other applications for multitasking, and a Fullscreen Mode for distraction-free viewing. Under the hood, Clapper relies on GStreamer for decoding and playback, which gives it broad codec and container format support consistent with whatever GStreamer plugins are installed on the host system, and it renders video using OpenGL for smooth, hardware-accelerated playback on both Intel and AMD graphics hardware. It supports both the older Xorg display server and the newer Wayland protocol, which matters for GNOME users since GNOME's own shell has been Wayland-first for years. Practical features include Media Player Remote Interfacing Specification, or MPRIS, support, which lets Clapper integrate with GNOME's system media controls and other MPRIS-aware desktop tools; configurable repeat options for looping playback; a full set of keyboard shortcuts for hands-off control; subtitle support with adjustable font and audio offset settings for fine-tuning synchronization; and support for streaming sources such as YouTube in addition to local files. Clapper is distributed primarily through Flatpak, the cross-distribution Linux packaging format, which is the officially recommended installation method and ensures consistent behavior across different Linux distributions regardless of their underlying package management systems; it is also available through the Arch User Repository for Arch and Manjaro users and is packaged in repositories such as Fedora's. The project has been associated with GNOME's broader application ecosystem and is commonly listed among recommended GNOME-native media players, reflecting its adherence to GNOME's human interface guidelines and its use of GNOME platform technologies, though as an independently maintained project it does not carry the backing of a company or foundation beyond the volunteer GNOME infrastructure it builds upon. Because Clapper is free, open source, and maintained without a commercial entity behind it, there is no pricing model, subscription, or paid tier to evaluate; its ongoing availability depends on continued volunteer maintenance by its developer and contributors rather than a business roadmap. It is best suited to GNOME desktop users who want a native-feeling, visually minimal video player that integrates cleanly with GNOME's design language, as opposed to users who need advanced editing, transcoding, or professional media management features, which fall outside Clapper's intentionally focused scope as a playback-first application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Design Philosophy and Technology Stack
paragraphs: Clapper is built with GTK4 for its interface, GStreamer for media decoding and playback, and OpenGL for rendering, all coded in GJS, GNOME's JavaScript environment. This stack ties it closely to the GNOME platform's design language and technical conventions.

Its interface intentionally omits a conventional title bar, relying instead on auto-hiding controls for preferences, mode switching, and window management, producing a clean, distraction-minimizing look, particularly in fullscreen or floating playback.

long_form_blocks.heading: Viewing Modes and Everyday Features
paragraphs: Clapper offers three distinct modes: Windowed Mode for standard desktop playback, Floating Mode for a resizable always-on-top window suited to multitasking, and Fullscreen Mode for immersive, distraction-free viewing.

Day-to-day functionality includes MPRIS integration for system media controls, configurable repeat options, keyboard shortcuts, subtitle support with adjustable font and timing offsets, and playback of streaming sources like YouTube alongside local files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Development Model and Availability
paragraphs: Clapper is primarily developed by one contributor, Rafostar, as a hobby project, which the project describes as resulting in steady but not rapid development. Community contributions supplement the core development work over time.

The application is distributed mainly through Flatpak for consistent cross-distribution installation, and is also available through the Arch User Repository and packaged in some distribution repositories such as Fedora's.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clappia is a no-code platform for building business apps and workflows. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clappia is a no-code application development platform designed to let non-technical business users build custom apps for operational workflows such as inspections, surveys, procurement, approvals, and field reporting, without writing code. The company was founded in 2017 by Ashutosh Thakur and Sarthak Jain, both alumni of the Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur who brought prior experience from established technology companies into building the platform. Clappia is headquartered in Bengaluru, Karnataka, India, specifically in the HSR Layout area of the city, and operates as a small, self-funded team of roughly twenty to twenty-five employees, a notably lean headcount relative to the scale of its customer base, which the company describes as more than 2,000 organizations and over 300,000 users worldwide, including Fortune 500 companies, government bodies, and small and medium-sized enterprises. While Clappia received early seed funding, reportedly including participation from the Axilor Accelerator around 2018, the company has since operated as a self-funded, profitable business without relying on major venture capital rounds, which the company and outside profiles point to as a deliberate strategic choice to preserve independence and control over its product direction. Functionally, Clappia positions itself as a platform for digitizing operational processes that would otherwise run on paper forms, spreadsheets, or fragmented point tools. Users build applications through a visual, drag-and-drop app builder that includes pre-built templates for common use cases, customizable data models, workflow and approval logic, role-based access controls, and reporting and analytics dashboards that give administrators and executives centralized visibility into standardized processes across distributed teams and field locations. The platform supports both mobile and web app creation from the same underlying app definition, which is particularly relevant for its core use cases in field operations such as inspections and surveys, where workers may need offline-capable mobile data capture that later syncs to central systems. Clappia also exposes API access for integrating generated apps with other business systems, and provides deployment management tooling for rolling out and updating apps across an organization. On pricing, Clappia follows a per-user, per-month subscription model layered on top of a free entry plan: the Free Plan allows initial evaluation and small-scale use, a Starter Plan is priced around 8 dollars per user per month, a Professional Plan is priced around 10 dollars per user per month, and an Enterprise Plan is available with custom, negotiated pricing for larger organizations with more complex requirements such as advanced security, dedicated support, or higher usage volumes. Some third-party pricing aggregators cite lower entry price points around 5 dollars per month, which likely reflects promotional pricing, older figures, or different plan configurations, so prospective buyers should confirm current numbers directly with Clappia before purchasing. Clappia is best understood as a competitor to other no-code and low-code business app platforms aimed at digitizing operational workflows, distinguishing itself through a relatively affordable per-user pricing structure, a strong focus on field operations and inspection-style use cases, and a lean, self-funded corporate structure that has allowed it to remain profitable while competing against better-funded rivals in the broader no-code market.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Clappia Does
paragraphs: Clappia lets business users build custom mobile and web applications for operational workflows, including inspections, surveys, procurement, approvals, and reporting, using a visual drag-and-drop builder rather than writing code.

The platform is aimed at organizations that want to move processes off paper forms and spreadsheets and onto a standardized, trackable digital system, with centralized visibility for administrators and executives across distributed teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Background and Growth
paragraphs: Founded in 2017 by IIT Kharagpur alumni Ashutosh Thakur and Sarthak Jain, Clappia is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, and has grown to serve more than 2,000 organizations and over 300,000 users, including Fortune 500 companies, government agencies, and small and medium businesses.

Despite this scale of usage, Clappia operates with a lean team of roughly 20 to 25 employees and describes itself as self-funded and profitable, having relied on limited early seed investment rather than sustained venture capital funding to grow the business.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Clappia Pricing Works
paragraphs: Clappia uses a per-user, per-month subscription model with a free entry-level plan for evaluation, a Starter Plan priced around 8 dollars per user per month, a Professional Plan around 10 dollars per user per month, and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.
Buyers should verify current pricing directly with Clappia, since third-party aggregators sometimes list different or promotional figures that may not reflect the standard published rate.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClassDojo is a classroom communication and engagement platform founded in 2011 by Sam Chaudhary and Liam Don, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company built its product around a simple premise: give teachers, students, and families a shared, real-time digital space to communicate and track classroom life, replacing fragmented paper notes, phone calls, and disconnected email threads. ClassDojo has grown into one of the most widely adopted education technology products in the United States, with the company reporting usage in the large majority of American K-12 schools, alongside significant international adoption. The platform's core, free offering centers on several connected features. A points-based behavior and skills tracking system lets teachers award or subtract points tied to specific behaviors or character skills, giving students real-time feedback and giving families visibility into classroom conduct. Class Story functions as a private, secure photo and video feed where teachers can share moments from the classroom with parents, similar in spirit to a social media feed but restricted to a specific class's families. Student Portfolios let students themselves contribute by uploading photos, videos, and work samples documenting their own learning. Underpinning all of this is a messaging system that lets teachers and families communicate directly, with messages automatically translated into more than 190 languages, a feature aimed at making the platform accessible to non-English-speaking families, which is particularly significant given the linguistic diversity of many American school districts. ClassDojo's business model is freemium: the core app, including messaging, points tracking, Class Story, and portfolios, is free for teachers, schools, and families to use. Revenue comes primarily from ClassDojo Plus, an optional premium subscription marketed to parents and families rather than schools, which unlocks additional features for tracking and engaging with a child's school experience; ClassDojo Plus is offered after a seven-day free trial at 4.99 dollars per month or 59.99 dollars per year. The company has raised approximately 221 million dollars in venture funding across multiple rounds since its founding, including a 1.6 million dollar seed round in January 2012, an 8.5 million dollar Series A in April 2013, a 21 million dollar Series B in April 2016, a 35 million dollar Series C in February 2019, and a 125 million dollar Series D in July 2022, with investors including General Catalyst and Y Combinator, the startup accelerator ClassDojo went through early in its history. As of the most recent independent reporting available, the company was valued at approximately 1.25 to 1.3 billion dollars and reported annual recurring revenue in the range of roughly 52 million dollars, with a team of around 301 employees as of 2023 figures. ClassDojo's growth trajectory reflects a broader shift in education technology toward tools that center family engagement alongside classroom management, rather than treating parent communication as a secondary feature bolted onto a gradebook or learning management system. Because the core product remains free for teachers and schools, ClassDojo has achieved widespread organic adoption driven largely by individual teachers signing up their classrooms, a bottom-up growth pattern common among successful education technology products, rather than relying solely on top-down district-wide procurement deals, although the company has also pursued official school and district partnerships over time. For anyone evaluating ClassDojo in 2026, the key facts to know are that it remains free for classroom use, monetizes primarily through an optional family subscription, and continues to be one of the most recognized names in K-12 classroom communication technology in the United States and internationally.

long_form_blocks.heading: What ClassDojo Does
paragraphs: ClassDojo connects teachers, students, and families in one digital space, combining messaging, a points-based behavior and skills tracking system, a private classroom photo and video feed called Class Story, and Student Portfolios where children can document their own work and learning.

The platform's messaging system automatically translates communications into more than 190 languages, making it accessible to non-English-speaking families and helping schools maintain consistent communication across linguistically diverse communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Business Model and Funding History
paragraphs: ClassDojo operates on a freemium model: the core app is free for teachers, schools, and families, while revenue comes from ClassDojo Plus, an optional subscription for families priced at 4.99 dollars per month or 59.99 dollars per year after a seven-day free trial.

Founded in 2011, the company has raised approximately 221 million dollars across five funding rounds from seed through Series D, including a 125 million dollar Series D round in July 2022, with a reported valuation around 1.25 to 1.3 billion dollars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Adoption and Scale
paragraphs: ClassDojo reports usage across the large majority of United States K-12 schools, making it one of the most widely deployed classroom communication tools in the country, alongside meaningful adoption internationally.

Growth has largely been driven by individual teachers adopting the free tool directly for their own classrooms, a bottom-up pattern typical of successful education technology products, supplemented over time by broader school and district-level partnerships.
